title: On The Job 2

jokes.text: Tonight's the night the losers get together to share their success stories. It's going to be a short evening.
jokes.setup: Introducing a gathering where people will share their job successes
jokes.punchline: Predicting it will be brief because they're all failures
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie cynicism - sets up his character while delivering a sharp prediction that proves accurate. Classic setup-punchline with economic delivery.

dialogue.speaker: Louie De Palma
dialogue.line: Alright, listen up! We're gonna go around and share our job successes. Who wants to start?

dialogue.speaker: Louie De Palma
dialogue.line: This should be brief — you're all failures.

jokes.text: I can't stick around. I got to get back to work. I just stopped by to have a beer and gloat.
jokes.setup: Louie claiming he has to return to work, seeming successful
jokes.punchline: Admitting his real purpose is just to gloat over their failures
jokes.explanation: Honest character moment - Louie's brutal honesty about his petty motivations is both funny and perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I gotta get back to work. Things are really taking off for me.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: But honestly? I just wanted to come by and see how you bums are doing. And let me tell you, it's even better than I hoped.

jokes.text: Hey, Louie, you forgot to sign your unemployment check.
jokes.setup: Louie just bragged about his success and having to get back to work
jokes.punchline: Revealing he's actually on unemployment
jokes.explanation: Perfect deflation of Louie's pretense. The economy is superb - one line completely destroys his facade. Great structure.

dialogue.speaker: Louie De Palma
dialogue.line: Yeah, I gotta get back to work. Very busy day ahead of me.

dialogue.speaker: Unknown character
dialogue.line: Louie, what work? You're on unemployment.

jokes.text: I didn't know Guys and Dolls was in town.
jokes.setup: Louie barges into a high-class Wall Street office in his typical attire
jokes.punchline: Comparing his appearance to a theatrical costume
jokes.explanation: Sharp cultural reference that captures the class clash. The theatrical reference works well for the setting.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: What's the matter? You never seen a guy in a yellow shirt and a loud tie before?

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Gray
dialogue.line: Not since the last production of 'A Streetcar Named Desire.'

jokes.text: Snap this picture. Future generations are going to want an exact record of the moment that you hired your greatest salesman. Say 'cheese,' Cheese.
jokes.setup: Louie's over-the-top confidence in getting hired
jokes.punchline: The wordplay on 'cheese' with the boss's name Mr. Gray (calling him Cheese)
jokes.explanation: Classic Louie bravado with a weak pun. The audacity is funny but the wordplay feels forced.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Mr. Gray, I'm telling you, I got this job in the bag. I'm the best driver this company's ever seen.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Gray
dialogue.line: It's De Palma.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Whatever you say, Cheese.

jokes.text: You don't know how to get dirty.
jokes.setup: Louie diagnosing the company's problems
jokes.punchline: The metaphor of 'getting dirty' as business strategy
jokes.explanation: Sets up Louie's philosophy perfectly. The 'dirty' metaphor works on multiple levels - both literal and figurative.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You know what this company needs? We gotta get dirty. Real dirty. That's how you do business.

jokes.text: I was born dirty, Mr. Gray, and every day I get dirtier... and dirtier!
jokes.setup: The 'getting dirty' speech building up
jokes.punchline: The escalating intensity and absurdity of claiming to get dirtier daily
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation that's both absurd and perfectly in character. The repetition and intensity make it memorable and quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Louie De Palma
dialogue.line: You think I'm dirty? I get dirtier every single day. Every day I wake up, I get dirtier. I get so dirty, I make a mud puddle look like a car wash.

dialogue.speaker: Louie De Palma
dialogue.line: My dirt has dirt. My dirt needs a shower. I'm so dirty, soap takes one look at me and surrenders.

dialogue.speaker: Louie De Palma
dialogue.line: I don't bathe in water anymore—water baths in me. I'm filthy. I'm a walking landfill. I make the sewers look spotless!

jokes.text: Trouble, Mr. Gray?
jokes.setup: Security arrives during Louie's intense 'dirtier' speech
jokes.punchline: Understated reaction to the obvious chaos
jokes.explanation: Perfect straight-man reaction that punctuates Louie's absurd moment. Simple but effective.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: ...and another thing, you're all a bunch of...

dialogue.speaker: Security Guard
dialogue.line: You're gonna have to keep it down.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: What? I'm just having a conversation here!

dialogue.speaker: Security Guard
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, have it quieter.

jokes.text: Go on. And dirtier!
jokes.setup: Security interrupting Louie's speech
jokes.punchline: Mr. Gray encouraging Louie to continue despite the chaos
jokes.explanation: Brilliant reversal - the boss is now intrigued instead of appalled. Shows Louie's pitch is working despite appearances.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I was just explaining to these gentlemen about the time I almost became a priest...

dialogue.speaker: Security Guard
dialogue.line: Sir, you need to come with us.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Gray
dialogue.line: No, no, let him finish. I want to hear this.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Yeah, see, I was in the seminary for three weeks...

dialogue.speaker: Security Guard
dialogue.line: Sir, we have to ask you to leave the premises.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Gray
dialogue.line: Come on, he's almost to the good part!

jokes.text: I'm telling you, Tom, they're a fly-by-night outfit.
jokes.setup: Louie badmouthing AT&T to a client
jokes.punchline: Calling the massive, established AT&T a 'fly-by-night outfit'
jokes.explanation: Good absurdity in calling AT&T unstable. Shows Louie's willingness to say anything to make a sale.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: AT&T? That fly-by-night outfit? Forget about it.

jokes.text: Hello there, sweetheart. I don't believe I've had the pleasure. We've met. That's not the pleasure I was talking about.
jokes.setup: Louie's attempt at smooth workplace flirtation
jokes.punchline: His crude clarification of what 'pleasure' he meant
jokes.explanation: Typical Louie crudeness. In character but predictable. The structure works but it's not particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: It would be my pleasure to help you with that.

dialogue.speaker: Female colleague
dialogue.line: That's very kind of you, Louie.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, let's just say it'd be more than just kind, if you know what I mean.

jokes.text: Tomorrow, they'll be cranking out Cheryl Tiegs by the dozens.
jokes.setup: Louie pitching genetic research stocks
jokes.punchline: The specific absurdity of mass-producing a specific model/actress
jokes.explanation: Creative absurdity with good specificity. The Cheryl Tiegs reference is perfectly 1980s and shows Louie's crude priorities.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Listen, I got a tip on a genetic research company. They're gonna clone people, make 'em identical. Perfect workers, perfect soldiers, perfect everything.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Clone people? That's insane.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Insane? It's the future! You know what they're working on now? They're gonna mass-produce copies of the same person.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Why would anyone want a bunch of identical people?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Why? I'll tell you why. They're starting with Farrah Fawcett. They're gonna make a million Farrah Fawcetts.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: A million Farrah Fawcetts?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Yeah! Think about it—one Farrah Fawcett, that's good. But a million Farrah Fawcetts? That's a gold mine!

jokes.text: If you're in the half that's still around do you want to be rich or not?
jokes.setup: Discussing potential apocalyptic consequences of genetic research
jokes.punchline: Casually suggesting wealth matters more than global catastrophe
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark logic that's perfectly Louie. The casual acceptance of apocalypse for profit is both horrible and hilarious.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You're worried about genetic research causing the end of the world? I got news for you - I'd rather be rich when it happens.

jokes.text: Hello, Mrs. Greer? Have you decided what to do with your husband's life insurance settlement yet? Well, I noticed in the obits this morning that he died last night.
jokes.setup: Louie calling someone about investment
jokes.punchline: Revealing he's calling a fresh widow about her husband's death benefits
jokes.explanation: Shockingly dark and perfectly timed reveal. The casual cruelty is appalling but undeniably funny in its extremeness.

dialogue.speaker: Louie De Palma
dialogue.line: Hello, Mrs. Henderson? This is Louie De Palma from Sunshine Cab Company. I'm calling about your husband's death benefits... Yeah, well, he's dead, so I figured you'd want to know about the money coming to you. What do you mean 'how did I get this number'? It's on the accident report! Look, lady, I'm trying to help you here. The company's gonna pay out, and I thought you'd want to know...

dialogue.speaker: Louie De Palma
dialogue.line: What? No, I'm not trying to be funny. I'm being a mensch! Most people wouldn't even call. They'd just let you find out from the insurance company like a schmuck.

jokes.text: Oh, oh, oh, well, well, um, I'll call back in a... you know, after the services. All right, in the meantime, think chemicals and electronics.
jokes.setup: Realizing he's called too soon after the husband's death
jokes.punchline: Still trying to pitch stocks even while apologizing
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation of the previous joke. Louie can't help himself from making the pitch even in this horrible situation.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, I didn't realize your husband just passed away. I feel terrible calling at a time like this.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: But before you go, let me just say that funeral expenses can really drain your savings. You should really consider a diversified portfolio of blue-chip stocks.

jokes.text: Hello, Myron Schwartz? This is your cousin, Louis Ginzberg. No, I was at your wedding. We met behind the chopped liver.
jokes.setup: Louie pretending to be someone's cousin
jokes.punchline: The specific detail of meeting 'behind the chopped liver'
jokes.explanation: Good specific detail that sells the con. The chopped liver reference feels authentic and shows Louie's creativity in lying.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Yeah, I'm your cousin. We met behind the chopped liver at the deli on Second Avenue.

jokes.text: Oh, well, is your mommy home? No? Well, do you know where your mommy keeps her checkbook?
jokes.setup: Louie discovers he's talking to a child
jokes.punchline: Trying to sell stocks to a minor and asking about mommy's money
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly horrible escalation. Louie's willingness to scam a child is both shocking and completely in character.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: So kid, how much you got in mommy's purse?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I got some hot stocks I can move for you right now.

jokes.text: You simply don't fit into our organization. I rushed you a little bit, didn't I? I'm sorry, I... I made a mistake.
jokes.setup: The formal firing speech
jokes.punchline: Admitting he rushed to the conclusion
jokes.explanation: Mild irony in the polite corporate speak. Functional but not particularly memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Gray
dialogue.line: I came here today to fire you. But now I realize I may have rushed to that conclusion.

jokes.text: Everybody makes noises when they eat. Everybody makes eating noises when they eat. Yours are not eating noises. Yours are not... human noises.
jokes.setup: Discussion of Louie's eating habits
jokes.punchline: Escalating from loud to inhuman eating sounds
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation with perfect repetition and build. The 'not human' conclusion is both funny and believable about Louie.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Gray
dialogue.line: Louie, do you have to eat like that?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Like what?

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Gray
dialogue.line: Like you're trying to demolish the sandwich.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: This is how I eat. You don't like it, don't watch.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: *loud crunching and slurping sounds*

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: *increasingly inhuman eating noises*

jokes.text: Oh, no, I certainly have no complaints about your ethics. You have no ethics.
jokes.setup: Louie asking if it's about ethics
jokes.punchline: The clarification that having no ethics isn't the problem
jokes.explanation: Brilliant economy and structure. The deadpan delivery makes it clear that Louie's lack of ethics is just expected.

dialogue.speaker: Louie De Palma
dialogue.line: Is this about ethics?

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Gray
dialogue.line: No, no. I have no ethics.

jokes.text: The problem is... we can't stand you. Everyone's complaining. It's nothing personal, you're just... disgusting!
jokes.setup: Building up to the real reason for firing
jokes.punchline: The brutal honesty that it's purely personal disgust
jokes.explanation: Good escalation with the contradiction of 'nothing personal' followed by personal disgust. Effective but predictable.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Gray
dialogue.line: You want to know the real reason I'm firing you? It's not about your work performance or any of that professional nonsense. The truth is, I find you personally disgusting.

jokes.text: What you call disgusting, I call selling!
jokes.setup: Being called disgusting
jokes.punchline: Reframing it as his business philosophy
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie moment - turning his worst quality into a badge of honor. Great economy and character consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Louie De Palma
dialogue.line: Disgusting? That's not disgusting, that's my business philosophy.

jokes.text: I couldn't agree more. [Steals something from the desk]
jokes.setup: Mr. Gray's speech about enriching relationships and profiting from the situation
jokes.punchline: Louie agreeing while literally stealing from the desk
jokes.explanation: Brilliant visual callback that perfectly caps the sequence. Louie takes the enrichment advice literally and steals something.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Yeah, yeah, that's beautiful. That's real beautiful.

jokes.text: So long, loser.
jokes.setup: The polite goodbye after the philosophical speech
jokes.punchline: Louie's crude final insult
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie exit line. Simple but effective character moment that undercuts any pretense of civility.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: The polite goodbye after the philosophical speech. Louie's crude final insult.

jokes.text: That's a nice outfit. Thanks a lot, but you got to understand, this is not what I do for a living. You just do it for fun?
jokes.setup: Complimenting Bobby's Easter bunny costume
jokes.punchline: Her assumption that he dresses as a bunny for fun
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment. The misunderstanding about his motivation adds to Bobby's embarrassment.

dialogue.speaker: Cynthia Beck
dialogue.line: What a wonderful Easter bunny costume! You must have so much fun dressing up like that.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Fun? Lady, I'm a cabdriver.

jokes.text: Well, I've always admired your jelly beans.
jokes.setup: Bobby gushing about admiring her work as a film director
jokes.punchline: Her deadpan response focusing on the Easter bunny's props
jokes.explanation: Sharp deflation of Bobby's pretension. The deadpan focus on candy instead of his acting is perfectly cutting.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Your work as a director is amazing. The way you compose scenes, the emotional depth you bring to every frame—it's incredible.

dialogue.speaker: Cynthia Beck
dialogue.line: Thank you. You know, I'm most proud of my work on the Easter bunny's props. Really nailed the cotton tail.

jokes.text: Well, I've always admired your jelly beans.
jokes.setup: Bobby compliments her film work
jokes.punchline: She responds by commenting on Easter bunny jelly beans
jokes.explanation: Nice absurdist touch - she's treating him purely as the Easter bunny character, not an actor.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Your film work is really impressive.

dialogue.speaker: Cynthia Beck
dialogue.line: Thank you. You know, I've always believed that the Easter bunny makes the best jelly beans.

jokes.text: I've come all the way from the enchanted lettuce patch just to spend Easter with you.
jokes.setup: Bobby trying to be a convincing Easter bunny
jokes.punchline: The absurd origin story of the 'enchanted lettuce patch'
jokes.explanation: Mild absurdity in the bunny mythology. Shows Bobby trying too hard to be creative with the role.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: You see, it all started when I was just a young bunny, hopping through the forest...

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: And I came upon this magical lettuce patch, guarded by an old wizard.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: He said, 'Young bunny, if you can solve my riddle, the lettuce is yours forever.'

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: So I said, 'Lay it on me, wizard.'

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: He says, 'What has long ears, a fluffy tail, and hops around on Easter?'

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: I looked at him and said, 'That would be... me?'

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: And that's how I became the Easter Bunny and got the enchanted lettuce patch.

jokes.text: And now, kids, in keeping with the Easter season the Easter bunny has a special surprise for you. He's going to perform a scene from the Broadway play Whose Life Is It Anyway?
jokes.setup: Setting up a special Easter surprise for children
jokes.punchline: Choosing a heavy drama about paralysis and death for kids
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdity - the complete inappropriateness of serious drama at a children's Easter party. Original and shocking.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: I'm setting up a special Easter surprise for the children.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: I'm going to show them a heavy drama about paralysis and death.

jokes.text: Now, you have to pretend that the Easter bunny's paralyzed from the neck down.
jokes.setup: Explaining the dramatic scene to children
jokes.punchline: Asking kids to imagine the Easter bunny is paralyzed
jokes.explanation: Peak absurdity - asking children to imagine a paralyzed Easter bunny for dramatic art. Completely inappropriate and hilarious.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Imagine the Easter bunny is paralyzed.

jokes.text: She was so impressed by my performance that she offered me a part. Really? Yeah. What part? The clown at her daughter's birthday party.
jokes.setup: Bobby claiming his performance impressed the director
jokes.punchline: The 'part' is just another children's party gig
jokes.explanation: Good deflation of expectations. The structure works well with the setup and disappointing reveal.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: The director was so impressed, he's giving me a real part in a real movie!

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: I start tomorrow at two o'clock.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: That's great, Bobby. What's the part?

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: I'm Bobo the Clown at the Feldman kid's birthday party.

jokes.text: 12,391 hairs on my head.
jokes.setup: Alex's boredom as a night watchman
jokes.punchline: Counting individual hairs out of desperation
jokes.explanation: Good illustration of extreme boredom. The specific number makes it funnier than just saying he counted hairs.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: I'm so bored I'm counting individual hairs out of desperation.

jokes.text: Oh, what the hell, let's make it an even number. 12,390.
jokes.setup: Having counted all his hairs
jokes.punchline: Pulling one out to make a round number
jokes.explanation: Good callback and escalation. The casual self-mutilation for mathematical satisfaction is absurdly funny.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Having counted all his hairs, he's pulling one out to make a round number.

jokes.text: This is how I look when I say, 'This is how I look when I talk.'
jokes.setup: Alex practicing faces in mirrors
jokes.punchline: The recursive description of describing his face
jokes.explanation: Clever meta-humor that shows Alex's deteriorating mental state. The recursive loop is intellectually funny.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: My face is like a map of Ireland... if Ireland had a drinking problem and made bad decisions.

jokes.text: Good evening, ladies and gentlemen! Welcome to The Alex Rieger Show, starring Alex Rieger.
jokes.setup: Alex's isolation driving him to create entertainment
jokes.punchline: Creating a show starring himself for an audience of one
jokes.explanation: Shows Alex's mental deterioration well. The narcissism is funny but the concept is fairly predictable.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: You know, I've been thinking. Maybe I should create my own show. A one-man production.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: I'll be the star, the director, the producer... everything.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: And my audience? Just me. Perfect. No critics, no bad reviews.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Finally, I'll have a standing ovation whenever I want one.

jokes.text: Our first guest this evening, ladies and gentlemen, is the greatest juggler in the world. And let's see if I get his name straight here: Alex Rieger!
jokes.setup: Introducing his first guest
jokes.punchline: The guest is also himself with over-the-top credentials
jokes.explanation: Continues the isolation theme. The pretense of not knowing his own name adds to the absurdity.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Ladies and gentlemen, please welcome my first guest tonight. He's a man of many talents, an expert in virtually every field, holder of seventeen doctorate degrees, Olympic gold medalist in three separate sports, and author of twelve bestselling novels. Please welcome... me.

jokes.text: I am now going to attempt juggling... backwards. With one leg.
jokes.setup: Alex attempting to juggle for his imaginary show
jokes.punchline: Adding impossible difficulty constraints
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy that shows Alex's delusion. The absurd constraints make it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Okay, now I'm going to juggle while riding a unicycle backwards on a tightrope.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Blindfolded. During an earthquake.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: While reciting the periodic table.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: And my hands are tied behind my back.

jokes.text: This is station Y.A.W.N. signing off after an evening of telecasting.
jokes.setup: Alex ending his imaginary TV show
jokes.punchline: The call letters Y.A.W.N. reflecting the boring content
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay that's self-aware about the boring nature of his performance. Good economy.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: And that's our show! I'm your host, Alex Reiger. Join us next week on Y.A.W.N. — Because watching paint dry is more entertaining!

jokes.text: It's just the first couple of weeks on this job that are kind of tough, you know? But once you've been at it 20 years like I have, boy, you're going to learn to handle it.
jokes.setup: Henry offering encouragement to Alex
jokes.punchline: Implying 20 years of this job hasn't driven him completely insane
jokes.explanation: Dark irony about job satisfaction. The implication that Henry is also crazy adds depth to the joke.

dialogue.speaker: Henry
dialogue.line: You're doing great, Alex. Just keep at it.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Thanks, Henry. Does it ever get easier?

dialogue.speaker: Henry
dialogue.line: Well, after 20 years, I'm not completely insane yet. So there's hope for you.

jokes.text: It's time now... for The Harry and Muffy Show! Yeah! Yes! Yes, yes, yes, yes, yes. Well, who are our guests tonight, Muffy? Harry... you are a sick man.
jokes.setup: Alex creating another imaginary show
jokes.punchline: Having a conversation with himself where he calls himself sick
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Alex's mental state. The self-awareness of his sickness while continuing the behavior is funny.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: You're sick, you know that?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: I know.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Really sick.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: I said I know!

jokes.text: Harry... you are a sick man.
jokes.setup: Alex playing both characters in his fantasy show
jokes.punchline: One of his personas calls the other sick
jokes.explanation: Excellent moment of self-awareness within the delusion. Alex recognizing his own mental state through his fantasy character.

dialogue.speaker: Alex (as Muffy)
dialogue.line: You're sick!

dialogue.speaker: Alex (as other persona)
dialogue.line: No, you're sick!

jokes.text: So okay, so cab driving is a stinky, lousy job that, uh... has no comforts and no dignity and no future, but on the other hand... would anyone like to help me with the other hand? On the other hand we stink at everything else?
jokes.setup: Alex trying to find the positive side of returning to cab driving
jokes.punchline: The group realization that their alternative is being bad at everything else
jokes.explanation: Perfect encapsulation of the episode's theme. The group's honest self-assessment is both funny and touching.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Look, I know driving a cab isn't glamorous, but at least I'm good at it. I'm excellent at it, actually.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Yeah, well some of us aren't good at anything.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: I tried being a boxer once. Worst decision of my life.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I attempted to be a singer. Let's just say the audience wanted their money back.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I was terrible at every job I ever had before this one.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: You see? We're all actually pretty lucky. We found something we're decent at.

dialogue.speaker: Danny
dialogue.line: Yeah... being a cab driver.

dialogue.speaker: All
dialogue.line: Yeah... being a cab driver.

jokes.text: On the other hand we stink at everything else?
jokes.setup: Alex looking for the positive side of cab driving
jokes.punchline: Someone provides the brutal honest answer
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback and completion of Alex's setup. Brutally honest and economical.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: There's got to be some positive side to cab driving.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Yeah, when you get home at night, you can take it off.

jokes.text: Take this job and shove it.
jokes.setup: The famous song lyric about quitting
jokes.punchline: Said while returning to the job they're describing
jokes.explanation: Simple irony using a well-known reference. Economical but not particularly original.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Take this job and shove it, I ain't working here no more...

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: My boss has gone and pushed me to the point of no return.

jokes.text: Take this job and shove it.
jokes.setup: Everyone deciding to return to cab driving
jokes.punchline: Someone quotes the famous anti-work song about the job they're returning to
jokes.explanation: Cultural reference that's somewhat ironic given context, but not particularly clever or original.

dialogue.speaker: Alex Rieger
dialogue.line: Well, I guess we're all back to driving cabs.

dialogue.speaker: Latka Gravas
dialogue.line: Yeah, it's back to the same old grind.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby Benson
dialogue.line: You know, this reminds me of that song... 'Take this job and shove it, I ain't working here no more.'

jokes.text: Hey... what is it we do? We're cab drivers, Jim. Ah, come on. No, really. Wow!
jokes.setup: Back at work, someone asking what their job is
jokes.punchline: The amazement at remembering they're cab drivers
jokes.explanation: Shows Jim's character (likely the spacey one). The genuine wonder at basic job information is absurd but character-appropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: What do you do for a living?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: I'm a cab driver.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: A cab driver? How long have you been doing that?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: About ten years.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Ten years? And you still remember?

tmdb_overview: In part two, the gang continues talking about their new job experiences.  Louie gets a job as a stock broker, after convincing the boss that his persistence and lack of morals will make him successful.  While this proves to be true, he is fired nonetheless, because his colleagues can't stand him.  Bobby takes a job as a bunny for children's party, but when he discovers that a kid's mother is a famous director, he acts out a monologue from ""Whose Life is it, Anyway,"" to the disappointment of the children.  Alex gets a job as a night watchman, which is dull until he aims the surveillance camera at himself and pretends to be on ""the Alex Reiger Show.""  At the end, Louie learns that the cab company is back in business, and everyone returns to their old jobs.
insight: Taxi's most consistent character comedy vehicle: 46 jokes in 28 minutes anchors Season 3's workplace absurdism.
